content.text: To design and deliver a coherent, rigorous, and culturally responsive curriculum that integrates Cambridge International Education standards with Indonesian national requirements, ensuring students develop both global competencies and a strong Indonesian identity while meeting all regulatory obligations.

items: Aligned to Cambridge learning objectives and Indonesian national curriculum standards
Clear learning outcomes for each subject and grade level
Assessment criteria are transparent and communicated to students
content.title: Guiding Principles

items: Logical progression from grade to grade (vertical alignment)
Connections across subjects (horizontal integration)
Skills and knowledge build cumulatively
Honors Indonesian values, history, and context
Embraces global perspectives and diversity
Prepares students as confident Indonesian citizens and global learners
Active learning, critical thinking, problem-solving emphasized
Student agency and voice valued
Differentiation for diverse learners (EAL, SEN, gifted)
Academic rigor balanced with wellbeing and character development
Breadth (exposure to multiple disciplines) and depth (mastery of core subjects)
Time for co-curricular activities, creativity, and rest

content.text: Eduversal Partner Schools follow the Cambridge International Education pathway, which spans ages 5-19 and aligns with international best practices. Wellbeing and Social-Emotional Learning:

steps: Sanford Harmony program integrated into Cambridge Personal, Social & Emotional Development curriculum
Explicitly teaches empathy, communication, and positive relationship building
Supports Profil Pelajar Pancasila competencies from early years

items: Subjects: English, Mathematics, Science
Approach: Inquiry-based, skills-focused
Learning Objectives: Detailed for each subject and stage (Stages 1-6 within Primary)
content.title: Stage 1: Cambridge Primary (Ages 5-11, Grades 1-6)

items: Emphasis on foundational literacy and numeracy
Science explores the natural and physical world through investigation
Cross-curricular themes (e.g., environment, health, citizenship)
Formative: Ongoing classroom assessment by teachers
Summative: School-based tests aligned to Cambridge objectives
Cambridge Primary Checkpoint (End of Grade 6):
External diagnostic tests in English, Mathematics, and Science
Provides benchmark against international standards
Not high-stakes; informs transition to Lower Secondary
Core Subjects: English, Mathematics, Science
Additional Subjects (School Discretion):
Global Perspectives (interdisciplinary thinking, research skills)
ICT (digital literacy and computational thinking)
Arts (Visual Arts, Music, Drama)
Physical Education
content.title: Stage 2: Cambridge Lower Secondary (Ages 11-14, Grades 7-8)

items: Builds on Primary; introduces more disciplinary depth
Prepares for IGCSE specialization
Develops independent learning and research skills
Formative: Classroom-based, teacher-led
Summative: School exams, internal assessments

content.text: Cambridge Lower Secondary Checkpoint (Recommended, End of Grade 8):

steps: External tests available in English, Mathematics, and Science
Administered in April/May (recommended) or September/October
Diagnostic feedback confirms IGCSE readiness
School policy: All students are strongly encouraged to sit Checkpoint exams unless exempted for documented reasons
Students receive counseling in Grade 8 (Term 2-3) for IGCSE subject selection
Sample IGCSE Subject Selection Form provided to students in Semester II of Grade 8
Refer to the "Cambridge Pathway \- Subject Selection" document for detailed subject combinations by career path (Engineering, Business, Medical)

items: Core Subjects (Typically Required):
English as a Second Language (ESL) or English Literature
Mathematics (Core or Extended)
Sciences: Biology, Chemistry, Physics (separate or Combined Science)
Elective Subjects (Students choose 2-7 additional subjects based on interest and capacity):
Humanities: Geography, History, Global Perspectives, Economics
Languages: Indonesian First Language, Foreign Languages (Mandarin, French, Spanish \- availability varies)
Business: Business Studies, Accounting
Technology: ICT, Computer Science, Design & Technology
Creative Arts: Art & Design, Music, Drama
Other: Physical Education, Additional Mathematics (for strong math students)
content.title: Stage 3: Cambridge IGCSE (Ages 14-16, Grades 9-10)

items: Students take 5-10 IGCSE subjects over two years
Balanced selection covering sciences, humanities, languages, and skills-based subjects
Subject-specialist teachers
Inquiry, problem-solving, and application of knowledge
Independent study and research skills developed
Exam preparation integrated (past papers, mock exams, revision strategies)
Primarily external exams (May/June at the end of Grade 10)
Some subjects include a coursework component (teacher-assessed, externally moderated)
Tiered papers for some subjects (Core vs Extended), allowing differentiation

content.text: Cambridge ICE (International Certificate of Education): Optional group award for students taking 7 IGCSEs across five subject groups:

steps: Group I: Languages (2 different languages required)
Group II: Humanities and Social Sciences
Group III: Sciences
Group IV: Mathematics
Group V: Professional and Creative

items: Advanced Subsidiary (AS) Level: One-year course (Grade 11), typically 3-4 subjects
A Level: Two-year course (Grades 11-12), typically 3-4 subjects
Students may take AS in Grade 11 and continue to full A Level in Grade 12, or take full A Level over two years
content.title: Stage 4: Cambridge AS & A Level (Ages 16-18, Grades 11-12)

content.text: Subject Offering (Schools offer a subset based on staffing and demand):

items: Sciences: Biology, Chemistry, Physics, Mathematics, Further Mathematics
Humanities & Social Sciences: History, Geography, Economics, Psychology, Sociology, Law
Languages & Literature: English Language, English Literature, Indonesian
Business: Business, Accounting
Creative & Technical: Art & Design, Media Studies, Computer Science, ICT
Aligned with university major and career goals
Universities often require specific A Level subjects for certain programs (e.g., Medicine requires Biology and Chemistry)
School counseling team advises students on strategic choices (Section 23)
University-preparatory rigor
Independent research, critical analysis, essay writing
Seminar-style discussions, presentations, debates
Extended projects or dissertations (for some subjects)
AS Level: Exams at end of Grade 11 (May/June)
A Level: Exams at end of Grade 12 (May/June)
Some subjects include practical exams (Sciences) or coursework (Art, certain humanities)

content.text: Cambridge AICE (Advanced International Certificate of Education): Diploma-level group certificate requiring:

steps: Core: Global Perspectives & Research (mandatory)
Group 1: Mathematics & Science (2 points)
Group 2: Languages (1 point)
Group 3: Arts & Humanities (1 point)
Optional project-based qualification for AS/A Level students
Develops independent research, project management, and presentation skills
Valued by universities as evidence of self-directed learning
Can be taken alongside AS/A Level subjects

content.text: Legal Requirement: All schools in Indonesia, including Cambridge schools, must deliver mandatory national subjects and fulfill Ministry of Education curriculum standards (Section 2.4).
Purpose: Instill Indonesian national identity, civic values, and understanding of Pancasila (the five founding principles of Indonesia). Content:

items: Pancasila Principles:
content.title: 1. Pendidikan Pancasila dan Kewarganegaraan (PPKn) \- Civics Education

steps: Belief in One God (Ketuhanan Yang Maha Esa)
Just and Civilized Humanity (Kemanusiaan yang Adil dan Beradab)
Unity of Indonesia (Persatuan Indonesia)
Democracy Guided by Wisdom (Kerakyatan yang Dipimpin oleh Hikmat Kebijaksanaan)
Social Justice (Keadilan Sosial)

items: Constitution and Governance: Indonesian governmental structure, rights and responsibilities of citizens
National Symbols: Flag, anthem, coat of arms, national holidays
Social and Moral Issues: Anti-corruption, tolerance, community service
Language of Instruction: Bahasa Indonesia
Periods per Week: 2 hours (Grades 1-12)
Teacher Qualification: Indonesian-certified PPKn teacher or social studies teacher with PPKn training
Assessment: School-based tests, projects, participation (not Cambridge-assessed)
Themes complement Cambridge Global Perspectives (citizenship, ethics, social issues)
Indonesian context enriches discussions in Cambridge History, Geography, Economics

content.text: Purpose: Develop proficiency in national language (reading, writing, speaking, listening), literature appreciation, and cultural literacy. Content:

items: Language Skills: Grammar, vocabulary, composition, comprehension
Literature: Indonesian poetry, novels, short stories, drama (classic and contemporary authors)
Communication: Formal and informal registers, presentations, debates
Media Literacy: Analyzing texts, critical reading
content.title: 2. Bahasa Indonesia \- Indonesian Language

items: Language of Instruction: Bahasa Indonesia
Periods per Week: 4-6 hours (varies by grade level)
Teacher Qualification: Indonesian-certified Bahasa Indonesia teacher
Assessment: School-based tests, essays, oral presentations, literature projects
All students graduate with strong Bahasa Indonesia literacy (equivalent to native speaker proficiency for Indonesian students; advanced proficiency for non-Indonesian students)
Supports university admission in Indonesia (Bahasa Indonesia required for national exams or admission tests)
Students develop biliteracy (English via Cambridge, Indonesian via national curriculum)
Translation and comparative literature projects bridge both languages

content.text: Purpose: Provide religious instruction aligned with student's faith, promoting moral values and spiritual development. Content (Varies by Religion):

items: Islam: Quran, Hadith, Islamic history, fiqh (jurisprudence), akhlaq (ethics)
Christianity (Protestant/Catholic): Bible study, Christian theology, church history, ethics
Hinduism: Vedas, Bhagavad Gita, Hindu philosophy, rituals
Buddhism: Tripitaka, Buddhist teachings, meditation, ethics
Confucianism: Confucian classics, ethics, family values
Other: If student practices different faith, alternative arrangement made (study independently or receive instruction from community religious leader)
content.title: 3. Pendidikan Agama (Religious Education)

items: Language of Instruction: Bahasa Indonesia (or language of religious texts with translation)
Periods per Week: 2-3 hours (Grades 1-12)
Teacher Qualification: Religious education teacher certified in respective faith
Grouping: Students grouped by religion for instruction (separate classes for Islam, Christianity, Hinduism, Buddhism, Confucianism)

content.text: Assessment: School-based tests, projects, memorization (e.g., Quranic verses for Muslim students), ethical reflection Respect for Diversity:

items: School provides qualified teachers for all faiths represented in student body
Prayer spaces available (e.g., musholla for Muslim students, chapel or quiet room for Christian students)
Religious observances accommodated (e.g., time for Friday prayers, fasting during Ramadan)
Ethical discussions in Cambridge subjects (Philosophy, Global Perspectives, Literature) informed by diverse religious perspectives
Respectful dialogue and interfaith understanding emphasized

content.text: Purpose: Connect curriculum to local culture, language, environment, or economic context as determined by provincial/district education office. Examples (Varies by School Location):

items: Javanese Language & Culture (Central Java, Yogyakarta)
Sundanese Language & Culture (West Java)
Balinese Arts and Traditions (Bali)
Local Environmental Studies (e.g., marine ecosystems in coastal regions)
Regional History (e.g., Jakarta history and development)
Traditional Crafts (e.g., batik, weaving)
content.title: 4. Muatan Lokal (Mulok) \- Local Content

items: Language of Instruction: Bahasa Indonesia or local language
Periods per Week: 2 hours (varies by grade)
Teacher: Local expert or certified teacher with relevant background

content.text: Assessment: School-based; often project-based or performance-based (e.g., cultural presentation, craft creation) Compliance:

items: Mulok subjects determined in consultation with local Dinas Pendidikan
Documented in the school curriculum plan submitted for operational license
Enriches Global Perspectives and Arts curriculum with local context
Students develop pride in local identity while engaging globally

content.text: Indonesian Ministry of Education (Kemendikbudristek) specifies minimum content standards and learning objectives for each national subject at each grade level, detailed in:

items: Kurikulum Nasional (Merdeka) (current framework as of 2024-2025)
Capaian Pembelajaran (CP) \- Learning Outcomes for each subject and phase
content.title: National Curriculum Standards by Grade Level

items: Curriculum maps for national subjects aligned to CP
Lesson plans reference national standards
Assessment measures progress against CP
Documentation available for inspection (Dapodik, Dinas visits)

content.text: Purpose: Ensure comprehensive coverage of both the Cambridge and Indonesian curriculum requirements, with clear progression across grades.
What is a Curriculum Map? A visual or tabular representation showing:

items: What is taught (topics, units, skills)
When it is taught (term, month, week)
How it aligns to standards (Cambridge objectives, Indonesian CP)
Why it matters (Schoolwide Learner Outcomes, Profil Pelajar Pancasila competencies)
content.title: Curriculum Map Structure

content.text: Example: Grade 10 IGCSE Biology Curriculum Map (Excerpt) Full Map:

items: Covers all units for the entire two-year IGCSE course (Grades 9-10)
Includes term breaks, mock exams, and revision periods
Shared with students at the start of the course (transparency)

content.text: Scope: What content is covered across the curriculum (breadth) Sequence: The order in which content is taught (progression) Purpose:

items: Ensure no gaps or redundant overlap
Show vertical alignment (Grade 7 Math builds on Grade 6; Grade 10 Chemistry builds on Grade 9 Science)
Facilitate teacher transitions (new teacher can see where the previous teacher left off)
content.title: Scope and Sequence Document

items: Subject-specific document (e.g., "Mathematics Scope & Sequence Grades 1-12")
Grade-by-grade breakdown of topics
Spiral curriculum approach: Topics revisited with increasing depth (e.g., fractions in Grade 3, 5, 7, 9 \- each time more complex)
Algebra builds from simple expressions (Grade 7) → equations (Grade 8) → quadratics (Grade 9) → advanced algebra (Grade 10-11)
Geometry progresses from basic shapes → advanced properties → trigonometry

content.text: Example: Grade 10 Weekly Timetable (40 periods @ 45 minutes each) (Demo Only) ![][image4] Notes:

items: Cambridge subjects \= 27 periods (66%)
National subjects \= 10 periods (24%)
P5 & PE \= 4 periods (10%)
Balanced but Cambridge-focused (prepares for IGCSE exams while fulfilling Indonesian requirements)
content.title: Integration: Cambridge \+ National Subjects in Weekly Timetable

content.text: Purpose: Ensure smooth transitions between grade levels and curriculum stages, avoiding gaps or excessive repetition.

items: End of each academic year (May-June)
Before start of new year (July-August)
content.title: Articulation Meetings

items: Teachers from consecutive grade levels (e.g., Grade 6 Primary and Grade 7 Lower Secondary teachers meet)
HODs and Academic Coordinators

steps: Review Outgoing Students:

items: What did Grade 6 students master?
What gaps or challenges persist?
Individual student needs (EAL, SEN) flagged for Grade 7 teachers

steps: Curriculum Handoff:

items: Grade 6 teacher shares final units taught, depth covered
Grade 7 teacher reviews planned starting point
Adjust Grade 7 curriculum if needed (e.g., if Grade 6 didn't complete a topic, Grade 7 includes a quick review)

steps: Assessment Data:

items: Checkpoint results (if taken) were analyzed for trends
Baseline assessment planned for Grade 7 (first 2 weeks of school) to confirm readiness

steps: Pedagogical Consistency:

items: Discuss teaching strategies, classroom routines
Ensure Grade 7 builds on Grade 6 approaches (e.g., if Grade 6 used inquiry-based science, Grade 7 continues)
Meeting notes with action items
Updated curriculum maps if adjustments have been made
Student transition profiles (for EAL/SEN students especially)
Orientation Program: Grade 6 students visit secondary campus, meet teachers, tour facilities (May-June)
Bridge Lessons: First 2 weeks of Grade 7 include review of key Grade 6 concepts (refresher, not re-teaching)
Social-Emotional Support: Homeroom teacher and counselor check in on adjustment (new environment, increased workload)
content.title: Transition Support for Students

items: Subject Selection Guidance: Counseling sessions in Grade 8 (Term 2-3) to choose IGCSE subjects
Summer Bridge Program (Optional): Intensive 1-2 week program in July for students who need extra preparation (e.g., strengthen English, review math fundamentals)
IGCSE Expectations: Workshops for students and parents on exam format, study skills, time management
University and Career Counseling: Begins in Grade 10 (Section 23)
AS Level Subject Selection: Based on university goals and IGCSE performance
Study Skills Boot Camp: Grade 11 orientation includes sessions on independent study, research, essay writing (A Level demands)

content.text: Purpose: Create connections across subjects so students see relationships and apply learning in authentic contexts.
Example 1: Grade 8 \- "Water: A Global Resource" Participating Subjects:

items: Science: Water cycle, properties of water, water pollution, purification
Geography: Global water distribution, access to clean water, case studies (drought in Africa, floods in Asia)
Math: Data analysis (water usage statistics, graphs, percentages)
English: Persuasive writing (letter to government on water conservation); reading (articles on water crisis)
PPKn (Civics): Rights and responsibilities regarding natural resources; Indonesian water management policies
content.title: Interdisciplinary Units

items: Students work in teams to research water issues in the Indonesian context (e.g., Jakarta flooding, rural access to clean water)
Create a multimedia presentation with proposed solutions
Present to the school community
Assessment: Each subject assesses the contribution from their discipline; team collaboration grade
Deeper understanding through multiple perspectives
Real-world relevance
Skills transfer (research, presentation, teamwork)
Economics: Trade, multinational corporations, economic interdependence
Geography: Global supply chains, environmental impact, urbanization
History: Colonialism and post-colonial economies, historical roots of globalization
Business Studies: International business strategies, cultural considerations
English Literature: Post-colonial literature (Chinua Achebe, Arundhati Roy)
Individual research essay on one aspect of globalization (topic choice based on interest)
Symposium where students present findings (TED-talk style)

content.text: Integration Across All Subjects: Projects (Section 2.4) are inherently interdisciplinary, involving multiple subjects and real-world issues. Example Profile Pelajar Pancasila Project: "Sustainable Living" (Grade 9, Term 1) Driving Question: "How can our school community reduce environmental impact and promote sustainability?" Subject Contributions:

items: Science: Investigate carbon footprint, waste management, renewable energy
Math: Calculate school's energy usage, cost-benefit analysis of solar panels
Geography: Study climate change and local environmental issues
PPKn: Explore government policies on environment, civic responsibility
Bahasa Indonesia: Write public awareness campaign materials
Art: Design posters and infographics for campaign
ICT: Create website or video documentary
content.title: Profil Pelajar Pancasila Projects

content.text: Timeline: 4 weeks (block schedule: 2-3 hours per week dedicated to project) Outcome:

items: Students develop action plan (e.g., start recycling program, reduce plastic use, organize tree planting)
Implement one initiative in school
Document and reflect on learning
Profil Pelajar Pancasila competencies (not subject grades): Bernalar Kritis, Kreatif, Bergotong Royong, Berkebinekaan Global
Rubric assesses process (research, collaboration, creativity) and product (quality of action plan, impact)

subsections.title: English as an Additional Language Integration

content.text: Challenge: Many Indonesian students enter Eduversal schools with limited English proficiency but must access rigorous Cambridge curriculum taught in English. Solution: Comprehensive EAL support model integrated into curriculum.
Eduversal adopts CEFR (Common European Framework of Reference) or WIDA (World-Class Instructional Design and Assessment) framework for EAL proficiency levels: CEFR Levels:

items: A1 (Beginner): Basic phrases, simple sentences
A2 (Elementary): Simple conversations on familiar topics
B1 (Intermediate): Understand main points of clear standard input, can describe experiences
B2 (Upper Intermediate): Interact with fluency, understand complex texts
C1 (Advanced): Fluent and spontaneous, understands demanding texts
C2 (Proficiency): Near-native; can understand virtually everything
content.title: EAL Proficiency Framework

items: Students should reach B2 minimum by end of Grade 10 (ready for IGCSE)
C1 by end of Grade 11 (ready for AS/A Level)
Students with lowest English proficiency pulled out of some Cambridge subject lessons for intensive English instruction
Focus: Survival English, basic academic vocabulary, foundational grammar
Gradual transition to mainstream as proficiency improves
content.title: EAL Support Models

items: EAL specialist co-teaches with subject teacher in mainstream classroom
Provides scaffolding (simplified instructions, visual aids, sentence frames)
Pre-teaches vocabulary before units
Small group support during lessons
Subject teachers trained in EAL pedagogy
Use strategies to make content accessible:
Visual supports: Diagrams, videos, graphic organizers
Simplified language: Chunk instructions, use clear sentence structures
Collaborative learning: Pair EAL students with proficient English speakers
Differentiated tasks: Provide options for different language levels (e.g., write paragraph vs. create labeled diagram)
Explicit teaching of academic vocabulary and discourse (e.g., compare/contrast, analyze, hypothesize)
Genre-based writing instruction (lab reports, essays, persuasive writing)
Reading strategies (skimming, scanning, annotation)
EAL proficiency assessed termly using standardized tools (e.g., WIDA ACCESS, Cambridge English Placement Test)
Progress tracked in student language profile
content.title: EAL Progression and Exit Criteria

items: Student reaches B2 level (CEFR) or WIDA Level 4-5
Can access grade-level Cambridge curriculum with minimal support
Continues to receive accommodations as needed (e.g., extended time, word bank) but no longer pulled out
EAL student profiles with proficiency levels and progress
EAL support schedules (pull-out, push-in)
Teacher training records (sheltered instruction, EAL strategies)
Student work samples showing language development

content.text: Principle: All students can learn and achieve high standards, but they need different pathways and supports.

items: Readiness-based: Adjust complexity (e.g., Grade 9 Math Extended vs. Core tier)
Interest-based: Offer choice in project topics (e.g., research essay on any aspect of WW2)
Learning profile: Provide materials in multiple formats (text, video, audio, hands-on)
content.title: Differentiation Strategies

items: Flexible grouping: Pair/group students strategically (mixed ability, similar ability, interest-based) depending on the task
Scaffolding: Provide graphic organizers, sentence starters, and step-by-step guides for students who need structure
Tiered activities: Same objective, different difficulty levels (e.g., all students analyze poem, but questions range from literal comprehension to thematic analysis)
Choice boards: Students select from menu of assessment options (essay, presentation, video, poster, model)
Varied criteria: Rubrics with multiple pathways to success (e.g., creativity, accuracy, depth \- students excel in different areas)
Screening at admission (learning assessments, parent questionnaire)
Teacher referral if concerns arise
Diagnostic assessment by a school psychologist or an external specialist
content.title: Support for Students with Special Educational Needs (SEN)

content.text: Individualized Education Plan (IEP) or Individualized Learning Plan (ILP):

items: Document student's strengths, needs, and goals
Specify accommodations and modifications
Reviewed termly with parents, students, and teachers
Extended time on tests
Preferential seating (front of class, away from distractions)
Note-taking support (copy of teacher's notes, peer buddy)
Assistive technology (text-to-speech software, graphic organizers)
Chunked assignments (break long tasks into smaller steps)
Reduced workload (fewer problems, shorter essay)
Simplified texts or alternate materials
Adjusted grading criteria (focus on key objectives, not all)
SEN students remain in mainstream classes with support (inclusion model)
Pull-out for specialized intervention only when necessary (e.g., reading remediation, speech therapy)
SEN register (list of students with identified needs)
IEPs/ILPs (individualized plans)
Progress monitoring data
Accommodation implementation logs (which accommodations used, effectiveness)
High performance on standardized tests (Checkpoint, EASE)
Teacher nomination based on classroom excellence
Student/parent request
content.title: Challenge and Enrichment for Gifted/High-Achieving Students

items: Pre-assess students; if already mastered content, allow to skip and work on extension activities
Example: Student tests out of Grade 10 Math review unit, uses time for enrichment (exploring advanced topics, independent project)
Subject-specific acceleration (e.g., Grade 9 student takes Grade 10 Math)
Early IGCSE entry (take some IGCSEs in Grade 10 instead of Grade 11)
Depth over breadth (explore topics in greater complexity)
Independent study projects
Mentorship with teacher or external expert
Science Olympiads (Physics, Chemistry, Biology, Math)
EduOS camps for intensive preparation (Section 3, Section 19)
Gifted/talented student register
Compacting and enrichment plans
Competition participation and results

subsections.title: Use of Technology and Blended Learning

content.text: Purpose: Enhance learning through strategic use of digital tools, while maintaining strong pedagogy and human connection.

items: Primary (Grades 1-6): Shared devices (computer lab, tablet cart) for specific activities
Secondary (Grades 7-12): Students may use personal devices (BYOD \- Bring Your Own Device) or school-provided laptops/tablets
School provides: Interactive Flat Panels (IFP) or projectors in all classrooms, reliable internet
content.title: Technology Infrastructure

items: EduNav: School information system for grades, attendance, communication, and assignments
Learning Management System (LMS): Google Classroom, Microsoft Teams, or Moodle for posting assignments, resources, and discussions
Assessment Tools: Online quizzes (Kahoot, Quizizz, Google Forms), EASE platform
Communication: Email, messaging apps (for parent-school communication)
At Home: Students watch instructional videos or read materials (introduction to new concept)
In Class: Active learning activities (problem-solving, discussions, labs, projects) with teacher support
Example: Grade 11 Physics students watch a video on Newton's Laws at home; in class, conduct experiments and solve complex problems collaboratively
content.title: Blended Learning Models

items: Setup: Multiple learning stations in classroom (teacher-led, collaborative, technology-based, independent)
Process: Students rotate through stations in timed intervals
Example: Grade 8 English \- Station 1: Teacher works with small group on essay structure; Station 2: Peer editing pairs; Station 3: Vocabulary practice on tablets; Station 4: Independent reading
Combination: Mix of face-to-face and online learning (especially useful for exam prep, remediation, or when student absent)
Example: Grade 12 AS Level students attend 3 in-person classes and 1 online asynchronous lesson per week (online: watch recorded lecture, complete practice problems; submit for feedback)
Preparedness: All teachers trained to deliver online lessons if campus closure required (health emergency, natural disaster)
Continuity: Curriculum continues via video conferencing (Zoom, Google Meet), LMS, digital resources
Equity: School provides device loan and internet data support for students lacking access
Students and parents sign AUP at enrollment
Rules for device use, internet access, digital citizenship
Consequences for misuse (cyberbullying, accessing inappropriate content, cheating)
content.title: Responsible Technology Use

items: Integrated into ICT, Global Perspectives, and homeroom lessons
Topics: Online safety, privacy, respectful communication, evaluating sources, plagiarism avoidance
Guidelines on ethical use of AI tools (e.g., ChatGPT, Grammarly)
Permitted: Brainstorming, editing, grammar check
Prohibited: Submitting AI-generated work as own without attribution; using AI to write entire essays or solve problems meant to assess student's understanding
Teachers design assessments less susceptible to AI misuse (process-oriented, oral presentations, in-class writing)
AUP signed forms
Digital citizenship lesson plans
AI usage guidelines (published in student handbook)
Technology integration examples in lesson plans

content.text: Purpose: Extend learning beyond the classroom, develop talents, foster community, and support SLOs and Profil Pelajar Pancasila competencies.

items: Frequency: 2 periods/week (all grades)
Content: Sports skills, fitness, games, health education
Inclusive: Adapted activities for students with physical disabilities or health needs
content.title: Co-Curricular Activities (Part of School Day/Curriculum)

items: Primary: Integrated into weekly schedule (music/art lessons)
Secondary: Elective IGCSE subjects (Art & Design, Music, Drama) or co-curricular classes
Scheduled library time (Primary) for book selection, storytelling, research skills
Reading challenges (all grades) to promote independent reading
Book clubs (student-led or teacher-facilitated)
Debate Club: Develop public speaking, argumentation, critical thinking
Math Club / Science Club: Explore topics beyond curriculum, prepare for competitions
Coding Club: Programming, robotics, game design
Model United Nations (MUN): Simulate UN debates on global issues
Literary Magazine / Yearbook: Student journalism and publishing
content.title: Extra-Curricular Activities (After School, Optional)

items: School Band/Orchestra/Choir
Drama Productions: Annual school play or musical
Art Studio: Open studio time for student projects
Photography / Film Club
Interscholastic Sports: Basketball, football (soccer), volleyball, badminton, swimming (compete against other schools)
Intramural Sports: House competitions, grade-level tournaments
Fitness/Wellness: Yoga, martial arts, dance
Student Council: Elected representatives organize events, voice student concerns
Community Service Club: Organize volunteer projects (visiting orphanages, beach cleanups, fundraising for charity)
Peer Tutoring / Mentoring: Older students support younger students academically or socially
Environmental Club: Sustainability projects, gardening
Cultural Clubs: Celebrate and share diverse cultures (e.g., Indonesian Culture Club, International Club)
Language Clubs: Practice foreign languages (Mandarin, French, Japanese)

content.text: Purpose: Intensive coaching for students competing in national and international science Olympiads (Biology, Chemistry, Physics, Mathematics, Astronomy, Earth Science). Structure:

items: Selection: Students identified through strong academic performance and interest
Training: After-school sessions (2-3x/week) \+ intensive camps (7-20 days during school holidays)
Coaches: Expert teachers (often with Olympiad medal experience themselves) or external specialists
Curriculum: Advanced problem-solving, beyond standard IGCSE/A Level content
Competitions: Students represent school (and Indonesia) at regional, national, international Olympiads
content.title: EduOS (Eduversal Olimpiade Sains) \- Specialized Olympiad Preparation

items: EduOS supplements (not replaces) regular science classes
Skills learned (creative problem-solving, resilience, teamwork) benefit all academic work
EduOS enrollment and attendance records
Competition results (medals, certificates, rankings)
Testimonials from students on impact

content.text: Note: EduOS is a separate program from the Eduversal Quality Management System (QMS). While QMS components (EASE, DTP, Appraisal Program) focus on school-wide quality assurance, EduOS is an optional, specialized enrichment program for students interested in science olympiad competitions.

items: All students complete minimum community service hours (varies by grade; e.g., 60 hours over 3 years for Grades 10-12)
content.title: Service Learning and Community Engagement

items: Service projects connected to academic content and Profil Pelajar Pancasila values
Examples:
Grade 7 Geography (Urban Issues): Students volunteer at local community center, interview residents about neighborhood challenges
Grade 10 PPKn (Civic Responsibility): Organize voter education campaign (mock election in school, public awareness posters)
Grade 12 A Level Biology (Health): Partner with clinic to deliver health education workshops in underserved community
Service learning log (hours, activities, reflections)
Supervisor signatures verifying participation
Reflective essays or portfolios showing learning from service
Service learning policy and hour requirements
Student service logs
Partnerships with community organizations (MOUs, project documentation)
Reflection portfolios

content.text: Context: Some Eduversal Partner Schools operate residential (boarding) programs, especially Islamic boarding schools (pesantren) integrating religious education with Cambridge curriculum.

items: Evening Study Hall: Supervised study time (2-3 hours) for homework, exam preparation, independent reading
Teacher On-Call: Subject teachers available for tutoring or questions during study hall
content.title: Additional Curriculum Components for Boarding Schools

items: Islamic Studies: Quran memorization (tahfidz), Hadith study, fiqh (jurisprudence), Arabic language
Daily Prayers: Time allocated for congregational prayers (Salat); Islamic studies integrated with character development
Islamic Values: Boarding culture emphasizes akhlaq (ethics), discipline, community living aligned with Islamic principles
Self-Care: Laundry, room cleaning, time management, budgeting (older students)
Conflict Resolution: Peer mediation training, restorative practices
Leadership Development: Student prefects, dormitory captains, event organizers
Enrichment Activities: Sports tournaments, cultural events, field trips, guest speakers
Home Visits: Scheduled breaks for students to visit families; international boarding students may stay on campus with special programming
House System: Students grouped into Houses (dormitories) with dedicated house parents/pembina asrama
Wellness Support: Counselors available for homesickness, adjustment issues, mental health concerns
Communication with Parents: Regular updates on student wellbeing, academic progress, social development

content.text: Challenge: Boarding schools must balance rigorous academic program with need for rest, recreation, and social-emotional wellbeing. Strategy: Weekday Schedule (Example \- SMP/SMA Boarding School): Weekend (Friday-Sunday in some schools; Saturday-Sunday in others):

items: Reduced structured time; more choice-based activities
Friday: Special assembly, Jum'ah prayer (for Muslim students), half-day classes or no classes
Saturday: Sports, clubs, outings, relaxation
Sunday: Preparation for coming week, laundry, personal projects
content.title: Curriculum Balance in Boarding Schools

items: Academic rigor maintained during school day
Evening study ensures homework completion
Adequate sleep (8 hours for adolescents)
Time for physical activity, social connection, spiritual growth

content.text: Purpose: Continuously evaluate whether curriculum is achieving intended outcomes (student learning, SLOs, Profil Pelajar Pancasila competencies) and make improvements.

1. EASE (Eduversal Achievement of Standardized Echievement): EASE is a core component of Eduversal's Quality Management System (QMS), providing standardized assessments four times per year to measure:

steps: Student achievement levels
Subject-specific mastery
Classroom teaching effectiveness
Student proficiency in specific topics
Cognitive thinking processes
content.title: Data Sources

content.text: Purpose: Digital evaluation platform supporting both Cambridge curriculum and Kurikulum Merdeka implementation, with immediate online results accessible to students, teachers, and administrators. Integration: EASE data directly informs curriculum review, teaching practice improvements, and SLO achievement measurement (cross-reference Section 6 for detailed assessment protocols). 2. Student Achievement Data:

items: Cambridge External Exams: Checkpoint, IGCSE, AS/A Level results (compare to previous years, international benchmarks)
ANBK (National Assessment): Indonesian national literacy and numeracy assessment (sample of students Grades 5, 8, 11)
School-Based Assessments: Unit tests, projects, presentations \- analyze pass rates, grade distributions, learning gaps
Teacher Reports: Teachers submit end-of-term reflections on curriculum pacing, units completed, adjustments needed
Supervision/Observation: Eduversal Supervisi visits or Principal observations note whether curriculum is being delivered as designed
Student Surveys: Termly surveys on engagement, clarity of instruction, workload balance
Parent-Teacher Conferences: Parents provide feedback on student experience, curriculum rigor, communication
Focus Groups: Sample of students/parents participate in discussions on curriculum strengths and areas for improvement
Professional Learning Communities (PLCs): Teachers analyze student work, discuss curriculum effectiveness, share best practices
Lesson Study: Teachers observe each other's lessons, provide feedback, refine curriculum delivery

steps: Data Compilation:
content.title: Curriculum Review Cycle

items: Academic Coordinator collects all achievement data, teacher reports, survey results
Identify trends: Which subjects/units show strong results? Where are students struggling?

steps: Analysis Meetings:

items: HODs and Academic Coordinator analyze data by subject and grade
Questions:
Are learning objectives being met?
Is pacing appropriate (too rushed or too slow)?
Are resources adequate (textbooks, labs, technology)?
Are there equity gaps (EAL students, SEN students underperforming)?

steps: Curriculum Adjustments:

items: Update curriculum maps (add/remove/reorder units)
Revise scope & sequence if needed
Identify professional development needs for teachers
Request new resources (textbooks, materials, technology)

steps: Approval and Communication:

items: Principal/GM reviews and approves changes
Communicate updates to teachers (summer PD session)
Update parent/student handbooks if significant changes
Annual curriculum review reports (by subject/grade)
Data dashboards (achievement trends, growth metrics)
Meeting notes from analysis sessions
Updated curriculum documents (maps, scope & sequence)
External evaluator or Eduversal Director of Education leads comprehensive audit
Review alignment to Cambridge, Indonesian standards, SLOs, Profil Pelajar Pancasila
Benchmark against best practices (other international schools, research)
Major revisions if needed (e.g., shift from separate sciences to integrated science at Lower Secondary; add new IGCSE subjects)
Coordinated with CIS/WASC accreditation self-study (Section 18)

content.text: Philosophy: Curriculum is not static; it evolves based on student needs, educational research, societal changes, and stakeholder input.

items: Pilot PBL units in Grade 7-8 (interdisciplinary projects)
Evaluate impact on engagement, critical thinking, collaboration
Scale to more grades if successful
content.title: Innovation Examples

content.text: 2. STEAM Integration (Science, Technology, Engineering, Arts, Mathematics):

items: Design challenges combining multiple disciplines (e.g., build sustainable housing model using science principles, engineering design, artistic presentation, math calculations)
Maker spaces equipped with 3D printers, robotics kits, craft materials
Explicit teaching of intercultural skills, global issues, perspective-taking
Virtual exchanges with schools in other countries (video conferences, collaborative projects)
Model UN, global debates, international service projects
Increased student choice in curriculum (especially Grades 10-12)
Independent study options for advanced students
Competency-based progression (students advance when mastery is demonstrated, not just by time)
Representatives from each department meet quarterly
Discuss curriculum issues, propose innovations, and review data
Recommendations to the Academic Coordinator and Principal
content.title: Teacher Voice in Curriculum Development

items: Teachers investigate specific curriculum questions (e.g., "Does flipped classroom improve Grade 10 Math outcomes?")
Collect data, analyze, share findings with colleagues
Implement successful strategies school-wide
Collaborative process: teachers plan lesson together, one teaches while others observe, debrief and refine
Improves curriculum delivery and teacher practice
Curriculum committee meeting minutes and recommendations
Action research reports
Lesson study documentation (plans, observation notes, reflections)

content.text: Universal Design for Learning (UDL) is a research-based framework for curriculum design that provides all students with equal opportunities to learn. Rooted in neuroscience and educational psychology, UDL recognizes that learners differ in how they perceive, comprehend, engage with, and express their understanding of information. Rather than retrofitting curriculum for individual students after challenges arise, UDL proactively designs flexible learning experiences that accommodate diverse learning needs from the outset. Core Philosophy: UDL shifts the mindset from "What's wrong with this student?" to "What's wrong with this curriculum?" When barriers exist, UDL asks educators to remove obstacles in the curriculum design rather than expecting students to overcome fixed barriers through sheer effort or remediation. Three Key UDL Principles:

steps: Multiple Means of Representation (the "what" of learning): Present information in varied formats
Multiple Means of Action & Expression (the "how" of learning): Provide options for students to demonstrate knowledge
Multiple Means of Engagement (the "why" of learning): Stimulate interest and motivation through choice and relevance
content.title: What is Universal Design for Learning?

content.text: 1. Supports Cambridge Rigour with Inclusive Access: Cambridge International Education emphasizes high academic standards and the development of transferable skills (critical thinking, problem-solving, communication). UDL ensures that all students—regardless of their starting point—can access these challenging curricula without lowering expectations. By designing lessons with built-in flexibility, teachers maintain rigour while removing barriers that prevent students from demonstrating their true capabilities. 2. Aligns with Indonesian Inclusion Requirements: Indonesia's national education framework (Kurikulum Merdeka) and Profil Pelajar Pancasila emphasize inclusive education and recognizing students' diverse potentials. UDL operationalizes these principles by ensuring that curriculum design honours kebinekaan (diversity) and supports all learners, including those with special educational needs (SEN), English as an Additional Language (EAL) learners, and students from varied socio-economic backgrounds. 3. Enhances Differentiation Already in Practice: As outlined in Section 5.9, Eduversal schools already implement differentiation strategies. UDL provides a systematic framework that makes differentiation more proactive, sustainable, and embedded in curriculum design rather than reactive accommodations made on-the-fly. While differentiation often focuses on modifying instruction for individual students, UDL builds flexibility into the curriculum from the start, reducing the need for constant adaptations. 4. Addresses the Needs of EAL Learners: With 40-70% of students in Eduversal schools being EAL learners (Section 5.8), UDL strategies—such as providing visual supports, offering multiple ways to express understanding, and scaffolding language—are essential for linguistic and academic access. UDL and the EAL framework work synergistically to support language development while ensuring content mastery. 5. Prepares for CIS/WASC Accreditation: UDL demonstrates the school's commitment to inclusive practices, responsive curriculum design, and evidence-based pedagogy—all of which are valued by CIS (Council of International Schools) and WASC (Western Association of Schools and Colleges) accreditors. UDL artifacts (planning templates, student work samples, curriculum audits) serve as compelling evidence of teaching and learning excellence.
content.title: Why UDL Matters for Eduversal Partner Schools

content.text: Guideline: Provide learners various ways to acquire information and knowledge. Why It Matters: Students process information differently. Some are visual learners; others learn better through auditory or kinaesthetic experiences. EAL learners may struggle with text-heavy materials, while students with dyslexia benefit from audio options. Presenting content in multiple formats ensures no student is disadvantaged by a single mode of delivery. Application in Cambridge \+ Indonesian Curriculum Context: 1. Visual Supports:

steps: Infographics and diagrams: For complex concepts (e.g., Grade 9 IGCSE Biology—cell structure diagrams, annotated images)
Mind maps and graphic organizers: To show relationships between ideas (e.g., cause-and-effect charts for Grade 7 History—factors leading to Indonesian independence)

Videos and animations: Cambridge resources often include multimedia; supplement with platforms like Khan Academy, YouTube educational channels, or locally produced Indonesian-language videos for national subjects
content.title: Principle 1: Multiple Means of Representation

content.text: Example—Grade 8 Lower Secondary Science (Forces and Motion):

steps: Text: Provide textbook chapter with definitions and explanations
Visual: Show animated video of Newton's Laws in action (e.g., collision simulations)
Kinaesthetic: Conduct hands-on experiment with toy cars and ramps
Audio: Offer recorded explanation for students who prefer listening or need additional language support
Chunk text: Break long passages into shorter sections with clear headings
Highlight key terms: Bold or underline vocabulary; provide glossaries in both English and Bahasa Indonesia
Sentence frames: For EAL students, provide sentence starters (e.g., "The main cause of \\ is \\ because \_")
Dual-language resources: Where feasible, offer Indonesian translations for challenging English texts (especially in early IGCSE years)
Tiered reading materials: Provide same content at different reading levels (e.g., original Cambridge text, simplified version, illustrated summary)
Pre-teaching vocabulary: Introduce key terms before the main lesson using visuals and examples
Concept anchors: Connect new content to students' prior knowledge or cultural context (e.g., teaching fractions using Indonesian food portions—gorengan, tempe—before abstract math)
Advanced: Cambridge textbook chapter with case studies from multiple countries
Intermediate: Simplified article with Jakarta urbanization example, guided questions
Beginner: Infographic showing rural-to-urban migration in Indonesia with images and short captions
Extension: Research paper excerpts for high-achieving students exploring urban planning theory
Text-to-speech software: Tools like Immersive Reader (Microsoft), Read\&Write, or NaturalReader allow students to listen to text
Interactive simulations: PhET Interactive Simulations for science concepts
Cambridge Digital Resources: Utilize Cambridge GO platform for interactive activities and multimedia content

content.text: Guideline: Provide learners alternatives for demonstrating what they know. Why It Matters: A student may deeply understand photosynthesis but struggle to write a coherent essay about it due to language barriers or learning disabilities. Another student might excel in hands-on experiments but find written tests intimidating. UDL recognizes that assessment should measure understanding, not just writing or test-taking skills. Application in Cambridge \+ Indonesian Curriculum Context: 1. Varied Assessment Formats:

steps: Written: Traditional essays, reports, lab write-ups
Oral: Presentations, debates, recorded explanations, podcast episodes
Visual: Posters, infographics, photo essays, annotated diagrams
Performance: Demonstrations, role-plays, experiments, building models
Digital: Videos, websites, digital portfolios, coding projects
content.title: Principle 2: Multiple Means of Action & Expression

steps: Option A: Write a 1,500-word analytical essay comparing Indonesian and Indian independence movements
Option B: Create a documentary-style video presentation with narration, primary source images, and analysis
Option C: Develop an infographic timeline showing key events, leaders, and outcomes with written annotations
All options assessed against the same Cambridge objectives (analysis, evaluation, use of evidence) but allow different expression modes
Assistive technology: Word processors for students with handwriting difficulties; speech-to-text for those who struggle with writing
Scaffolded templates: Graphic organizers, essay frames, lab report templates for students who need structure
Collaboration options: Allow students to work in pairs/groups for certain tasks (with individual accountability measures)
Learning journals: Students reflect on their learning process, not just final products
Drafts and revisions: Emphasize growth through multiple iterations; provide formative feedback
Choice boards: Offer students menu of assignment options aligned to learning objectives

content.text: Example—Grade 6 Cambridge Primary Science (Environmental Change): Students demonstrate understanding of human impact on environment through one of:

steps: Written Report: Research and write a report on deforestation in Kalimantan with proposed solutions
Poster Campaign: Design educational posters for school corridors promoting waste reduction
Model Building: Create a diorama showing "before and after" environmental change
Digital Presentation: Make a slide deck with images, data charts, and action steps (All assessed using rubric covering: scientific accuracy, evidence use, proposed solutions, creativity)
Word banks: Provide key vocabulary lists for essay writing
Sentence starters: "In my opinion, \\", "Evidence suggests that \\", "This is significant because \_"
Oral presentation option: For students more confident speaking than writing (especially early EAL)
Translation allowances: Permit bilingual dictionaries or glossaries during assessments (where appropriate and compliant with Cambridge exam regulations)

content.text: Guideline: Tap into learners' interests, offer appropriate challenges, and increase motivation. Why It Matters: Engagement is the gateway to learning. Students disengage when content feels irrelevant, too easy, or insurmountably difficult. UDL promotes engagement by connecting curriculum to real-world contexts, offering choices, and creating a supportive learning environment where mistakes are part of growth. Application in Cambridge \+ Indonesian Curriculum Context: 1. Relevance and Real-World Connection:

steps: Local context: Frame Cambridge content through Indonesian examples (e.g., Grade 9 IGCSE Economics—study Indonesian small businesses, not just Western corporations)
Current events: Link lessons to news (e.g., Grade 10 IGCSE Geography—analyze Jakarta flooding in context of climate change)
Cultural hooks: Use Indonesian stories, heroes, or cultural practices as entry points (e.g., Grade 7 English literature—compare Western fairytales with Indonesian folktales)
content.title: Principle 3: Multiple Means of Engagement

content.text: Example—Grade 8 PPKn (Civics) \+ Cambridge Global Perspectives Integration: Topic: Democracy and Civic Participation

steps: Hook: Discuss recent Indonesian elections; students share family voting experiences
Cambridge GP Connection: Research democratic systems globally; compare to Indonesia
Action: Design campaign for increasing youth voter registration (real-world impact)
Topic choice: Within a unit, let students choose focus area (e.g., Chemistry unit on acids/bases—students pick whether to research industrial uses, environmental impacts, or household applications)
Learning pathways: Offer "must-do" core activities \+ "may-do" extension options
Goal-setting: Students set personal learning goals at start of unit; reflect on progress at end
Low floor, high ceiling tasks: Design activities where all students can enter (low floor) but advanced students can extend far (high ceiling)
Scaffolding for struggling learners: Provide graphic organizers, worked examples, peer mentors
Enrichment for advanced learners: Offer open-ended questions, independent research, leadership roles
All students (core): Solve standard quadratic equations using formula; complete practice problems
Struggling learners (scaffold): Start with visual models (area of rectangles), use step-by-step checklist, work in pairs
Advanced learners (extension): Explore quadratic inequalities, graph complex functions, create real-world application problems for peers
Peer teaching: Students explain concepts to classmates (reinforces own understanding)
Think-Pair-Share: Individual thinking time → discuss with partner → share with class (supports EAL learners who benefit from rehearsing ideas)
Group projects: Assign roles based on strengths; rotate roles to develop new skills
Frequent formative feedback: Focus on progress, not just accuracy ("You're improving at analyzing cause-and-effect. Next, try adding more evidence.")
Mistakes as learning opportunities: Normalize errors; use them as teaching moments
Self-assessment and reflection: Students identify strengths and set improvement goals

content.text: When designing or reviewing a unit/scheme of work, use this checklist to ensure UDL principles are embedded: Representation (How is content presented?)

steps: Content available in at least 2 formats (e.g., text \+ video, lecture \+ visual diagram)
Key vocabulary pre-taught and supported with glossaries, visuals, or translations
Complex texts have simplified versions or scaffolds (graphic organizers, sentence frames)
Real-world examples and cultural connections included (Indonesian context where relevant)
Technology tools integrated to enhance access (e.g., text-to-speech, interactive simulations)
content.title: UDL Unit Planning Checklist

content.text: Action & Expression (How do students demonstrate learning?)

steps: At least 2 assessment options provided (e.g., essay, presentation, project)
Rubrics/success criteria shared with students at start of unit
Scaffolding tools available (templates, checklists, worked examples)
Opportunities for revision/improvement based on feedback
Assistive technology encouraged (word processors, graphic design tools, audio recording)
Lesson includes a compelling hook or real-world problem
Students have some choice in topics, activities, or assessment format
Task is appropriately challenging (differentiated for readiness levels)
Collaborative learning opportunities included
Connection to students' interests, culture, or lived experiences
Formative feedback plan in place to support growth mindset
Representation: Use simulations (PhET), lab videos, 3D models, annotated diagrams
Action & Expression: Offer lab report options (traditional write-up, video demonstration, poster with findings); allow data presentation in tables, graphs, or infographics
Engagement: Frame units as investigations (e.g., "How can we reduce plastic waste in our school?"—chemistry of polymers); student-led inquiry projects
content.title: Subject-Specific UDL Strategies

steps: Representation: Use manipulatives (physical or digital), real-world contexts (money, sports statistics), visual models (number lines, area models)
Action & Expression: Multiple solution pathways allowed; students explain thinking orally or in writing; use of calculators/technology for complex computations
Engagement: Gamified practice (Kahoot, math challenges); application projects (design a budget, analyze sports data); student-created problems
Representation: Primary sources in varied formats (texts, images, videos, oral histories); timelines, maps, infographics
Action & Expression: Essays, presentations, documentaries, debates, historical role-plays, museum exhibit designs
Engagement: Local case studies (Indonesian history, Jakarta urbanization, ASEAN economics); current events integration; perspective-taking activities (write from viewpoint of historical figure)
Representation: Texts at varied reading levels; audio books; visual storytelling; bilingual resources for EAL learners
Action & Expression: Mix of writing, speaking, listening assessments; creative projects (podcasts, video scripts, comic strips); oral presentations with visual aids
Engagement: Choice in reading materials (interest-based book clubs); authentic communication tasks (letters, interviews, debates); cultural exploration (Indonesian vs. Western literature)
Representation: Demonstrate techniques through live modeling, videos, exemplars
Action & Expression: Multiple project options within learning objectives; peer critique sessions; portfolio-based assessment
Engagement: Student choice in themes/topics; connection to personal identity or cultural background; public exhibitions/performances

content.text: When choosing or creating resources, ensure they support UDL: Texts and Readings:

steps: Flexibility: Can text be provided in multiple formats (print, digital, audio)?
Readability: Is vocabulary age-appropriate? Are there supports for EAL learners?
Cultural relevance: Does content reflect diverse perspectives, including Indonesian context?
content.title: Resource Selection Criteria

steps: Accessibility: Are captions/subtitles available (English and Bahasa Indonesia if possible)?
Length: Are videos appropriately chunked (5-10 minutes) to maintain attention?
Quality: Is production clear, engaging, and aligned with learning objectives?
User-friendly: Can students operate tools with minimal instruction?
Accessible: Do tools work on various devices (laptops, tablets, smartphones)?
Supportive of learning: Does technology enhance understanding or just add novelty?
Options: Does assessment allow different demonstration modes (written, oral, visual, performance)?
Clear criteria: Are rubrics or success criteria provided in student-friendly language?
Formative opportunities: Can students receive feedback and revise before final submission?

content.text: UDL does not lower standards; it maintains rigorous Cambridge objectives while increasing access: Cambridge Assessment Objective (AO) Alignment:

steps: AO1 (Knowledge & Understanding): UDL ensures all students access content through varied representation; demonstrated through flexible expression modes
AO2 (Application): UDL tasks require real-world application (e.g., solving authentic problems, creating products for genuine audiences)
AO3 (Analysis & Evaluation): UDL supports higher-order thinking through scaffolding (graphic organizers for analysis, rubrics breaking down evaluation criteria)
content.title: Integration with Cambridge Assessment Objectives

steps: AO1 (Understanding): All students read the poem; some listen to audio version, others access annotated text with glossary
AO2 (Analysis of language/structure): All students analyze poetic devices; some use color-coding to identify metaphors/similes, others create visual maps

AO3 (Personal response): All students respond to poem; choice of essay, oral presentation, or creative response (poem rewrite with analysis) All options assessed against same Cambridge criteria; UDL provides pathways to demonstrate proficiency

content.text: As outlined in Section 5.8, 40-70% of Eduversal students are EAL learners. UDL principles are particularly powerful for supporting language development while ensuring content access.
content.title: UDL for EAL Learners

content.text: 1. Multiple Means of Representation → Language Input Support

steps: Visual supports: EAL learners rely heavily on images, diagrams, and gestures when verbal explanations are unclear
Text \+ audio combination: Listening while reading helps with pronunciation and comprehension
Simplified language versions: Provide same content with reduced linguistic complexity (shorter sentences, common vocabulary)
L1 (first language) support: Where feasible, provide Indonesian translations of key concepts (especially in early stages)
content.title: How UDL Specifically Supports EAL Learners

steps: All students: Read textbook chapter on climate zones (Cambridge resource)
EAL support: Provide infographic with climate types, temperature/rainfall charts, and example countries (including Indonesia) labeled in both English and Bahasa Indonesia
Additional support: Show video with narration; pause to explain key terms; students create visual vocabulary journal

content.text: 2. Multiple Means of Action & Expression → Language Output Flexibility

steps: Oral before written: Allow EAL learners to explain ideas verbally before writing (reduces cognitive load)
Visual demonstrations: Students can show understanding through diagrams, models, or performances when language is developing
Sentence frames and templates: Scaffold writing with structures (reduces struggle with grammar while focusing on content)
Extended time: Processing and producing in a second language takes longer; build in time buffers
Standard: Written lab report explaining experiment, results, and conclusion
UDL for EAL: Offer options:
Traditional written report with sentence frames ("We observed that \\ because \\")
Oral explanation (recorded video) \+ annotated data table
Poster with labeled diagrams, brief captions, and peer presentation
All options must demonstrate understanding of scientific concepts and use of evidence (Cambridge AO1, AO2)

content.text: 3. Multiple Means of Engagement → Language Learning Motivation

steps: Low-anxiety environment: UDL reduces fear of failure; mistakes are expected and used for learning
Culturally relevant content: Using Indonesian examples increases engagement and comprehension
Collaborative learning: Pair EAL learners with patient, proficient English speakers for peer support
Strengths-based approach: Celebrate progress in English while valuing students' multilingualism
Pull-Out EAL (A1/A2 beginners): UDL principles guide intensive English lessons (visual vocab, games, songs, TPR—Total Physical Response)
Push-In EAL (B1/B2 intermediate): UDL is the tool for mainstream teachers to make content accessible (visuals, sentence frames, flexible assessment)
Sheltered Instruction (All levels): UDL \= Sheltered Instruction in action (explicit language teaching \+ content mastery)
content.title: Connection to Existing EAL Framework (Section 5.8)

content.text: UDL Tools for Different CEFR Levels:

headers: CEFR Level UDL Representation UDL Action & Expression UDL Engagement
rows: A1 (Beginner)
Extensive visuals, gestures, L1 support, simplified texts
Drawings, labeling, single-word responses, oral demonstrations
Highly scaffolded, games, songs, concrete tasks
A2 (Elementary)
Visuals \+ simple English text, audio support, glossaries
Short sentences, fill-in-blanks, simple paragraphs, oral \+ written mix
Relatable topics, partner work, low-stakes practice
B1 (Intermediate)
Some authentic materials with scaffolds, graphic organizers
Paragraphs, presentations with notes, structured essays
Choice in topics, cultural connections, peer collaboration
B2 (Upper Intermediate)
Mostly authentic Cambridge materials \+ selective scaffolds
Essays, reports, presentations with minimal frames
Student-led projects, debates, real-world applications
C1 (Advanced)
Full Cambridge curriculum, extension materials
Independent research, sophisticated writing, nuanced analysis
Leadership roles, mentoring emerging EAL students

steps: Vocabulary preview: Introduce key terms with images, definitions, examples (UDL Representation)
Background knowledge activation: Connect new content to students' prior knowledge or cultural context (UDL Engagement)
Set language objectives: Clarify both content and language goals (e.g., "Today you will learn about ecosystems AND use comparative adjectives")
content.title: Practical Scaffolding Strategies Using UDL Principles

steps: Multimodal instruction: Combine verbal explanation \+ written text \+ visual diagram (UDL Representation)
Chunking and pausing: Break instruction into short segments; pause for processing and questions
Cooperative structures: Think-Pair-Share, jigsaw activities (UDL Engagement)
Comprehension checks: Quick formative assessments (thumbs up/down, exit tickets, mini-quizzes)
Differentiated practice: Tiered assignments based on language proficiency (UDL Action & Expression)
Flexible assessment: Offer options for demonstrating learning (written, oral, visual) (UDL Action & Expression)
Feedback focus: Comment on content understanding AND language development; prioritize clarity over grammatical perfection in early stages

content.text: Phase 1: Awareness and Introduction (Term 1, Year 1) Goal: Build foundational understanding of UDL among all staff Actions:

steps: August PD Week: 3-hour UDL workshop for all teachers (introduce 3 principles, why it matters, examples)
September: Heads of Department (HODs) attend deeper UDL training (full-day or 2-part session); begin UDL audit of current curriculum maps
October-December: Teachers choose 1 unit to redesign using UDL checklist; share in department meetings
content.title: Timeline for Rolling Out UDL in Curriculum Design

steps: Workshop attendance records
UDL-infused unit plans (at least 1 per teacher)
Department meeting notes discussing UDL implementation

content.text: Phase 2: Systematic Integration (Terms 2-3, Year 1 \+ Year 2) Goal: Embed UDL into all curriculum planning documents and teaching practice Actions:

steps: January (Year 1): Update school-wide unit planning template to include UDL checklist
February-March: PLCs focus on UDL (share strategies, peer observation, collaborative planning)
April-June: HODs review all schemes of work for UDL alignment; identify gaps
Year 2: All new units designed with UDL from outset; continue refining existing units
Updated unit planning templates with UDL sections
Peer observation notes highlighting UDL strategies
HOD curriculum review reports with UDL criteria

content.text: Phase 3: Quality Assurance and Continuous Improvement (Year 2 onward) Goal: Ensure UDL is standard practice; refine based on data Actions:

steps: Ongoing: Principals and HODs include UDL in lesson observations and walkthroughs
Termly: Analyze student achievement data disaggregated by EAL level, SEN status—are gaps closing?
Annually: Survey teachers and students on UDL implementation and impact
Ongoing: Celebrate and share UDL success stories (teacher spotlights, student testimonials)
Lesson observation data showing UDL strategy use
Achievement gap analysis (comparison of UDL-rich vs. traditional units)
Teacher and student feedback surveys
Case studies or video vignettes of exemplary UDL practice
UDL 101: What is UDL? Why does it matter? The 3 principles explained with examples
Differentiation vs. UDL: Clarify relationship; UDL as proactive, curriculum-embedded differentiation
Hands-on practice: Redesign a sample lesson using UDL checklist; share and critique in small groups
content.title: Training Needs for Heads of Department and Teachers

steps: Subject-specific UDL workshops: Science teachers explore UDL for labs; language teachers for literacy
Lesson study with UDL focus: Observe, analyze, and refine lessons specifically for UDL implementation
Technology integration: Training on assistive tech tools (text-to-speech, Immersive Reader, graphic design apps)
EAL \+ UDL: Special sessions for mainstream teachers on UDL strategies for language learners
Curriculum audit tools: How to review schemes of work for UDL alignment
Coaching conversations: How to guide teachers in improving UDL practice (strengths-based feedback)
Data analysis: Using achievement data to assess UDL effectiveness; identifying students still struggling despite UDL
CAST (Center for Applied Special Technology): www.cast.org—UDL Guidelines, examples, webinars
UDL on Campus: Online courses and certification programs
Cambridge Professional Development: Workshops on inclusive teaching practices

content.text: HOD Curriculum Review Protocol (Termly): Step 1: Select Sample Units

steps: Review 2-3 units per term from each teacher in the department
Prioritize units where students historically struggle or that are new to curriculum
content.title: Quality Assurance: Reviewing Schemes of Work for UDL Alignment

content.text: Step 2: Apply UDL Audit Tool Use this rubric to rate each unit (1 \= Not Evident, 2 \= Developing, 3 \= Proficient, 4 \= Exemplary):

headers: UDL Component 1 \- Not Evident 2 \- Developing 3 \- Proficient 4 \- Exemplary
rows: Multiple Means of Representation
Content presented in single format only
Content in 2 formats; minimal scaffolding for EAL/SEN
Content in 3+ formats; clear scaffolds; some cultural relevance
Content highly accessible; extensive scaffolds; culturally responsive; technology-enhanced
Multiple Means of Action & Expression
Single assessment type (e.g., only written test)
2 assessment options OR scaffolding for single format
2+ assessment options aligned to objectives; clear rubrics; some scaffolds
3+ diverse options; extensive scaffolding; assistive tech integrated; formative feedback loops
Multiple Means of Engagement
Generic tasks; little relevance or choice
Some relevance or choice; tasks mostly engaging
Clear real-world connections; student choice; appropriate challenge
Highly engaging; strong cultural/real-world links; student agency; social-emotional support

steps: Strengths: What UDL elements are strong? Celebrate examples.
Growth areas: Where can UDL be enhanced? Provide specific suggestions.
Action plan: Teacher and HOD agree on 1-2 UDL improvements for next unit revision.
Classroom observation to see UDL in practice
Check student work samples: Are diverse learners succeeding?
Revisit unit in next review cycle to assess improvements
Review HOD audit summaries (aggregate data: What % of units are UDL-proficient?)
Celebrate progress publicly (staff meetings, newsletters)
Allocate resources (technology, training, planning time) to support UDL growth
Include UDL in teacher appraisal criteria (Section 7)
UDL-Enhanced Unit Planning Template: Standard template with UDL checklist embedded
Annotated Schemes of Work: Examples showing how UDL is applied in curriculum design across subjects and grade levels
UDL Curriculum Audit Reports: HOD reviews documenting UDL alignment, strengths, and growth areas
content.title: Evidence/Artifacts

steps: Differentiated Resource Library: Collection of texts at varied reading levels, visual supports, audio recordings, videos
Technology Tools Inventory: List of assistive tech and apps available school-wide (e.g., text-to-speech, graphic organizers, design tools)
Scaffolding Toolkits: Subject-specific resources (sentence frames for English, graphic organizers for History, worked examples for Math)
Choice-Based Projects: Examples of students demonstrating same learning objective through different modes (essay vs. presentation vs. poster)
Scaffolded Assignments: Work showing progression from heavily scaffolded (beginner EAL) to independent (proficient English)
Student Reflections: Journals or exit tickets where students comment on UDL options that helped them learn
Training Agendas and Materials: Workshop slides, handouts, activity guides from UDL PD sessions
Lesson Study Documentation: Plans, observation notes, reflections focused on UDL implementation
Teacher Self-Assessment: UDL reflection tools completed by teachers (e.g., "Which UDL principles am I strong in? Where do I need growth?")
Achievement Data Disaggregated: Compare performance of EAL, SEN, and mainstream students in UDL-designed units vs. traditional units
Student Engagement Surveys: Feedback on lesson interest, relevance, and accessibility
Parent Feedback: Surveys or focus group notes on perceptions of inclusive teaching practices
Criterion 2.3 (Curriculum Design): "The curriculum is designed to be inclusive and accessible to all students."
UDL Evidence: Curriculum maps with UDL checklist; diverse assessment options; scaffolding resources
Criterion 2.4 (Learning Resources): "Resources support the curriculum and are accessible to all students."
UDL Evidence: Multi-format materials; assistive technology inventory; differentiated resource library
content.title: CIS/WASC Accreditation Standards

steps: Criterion 3.2 (Differentiation): "Teachers differentiate instruction to meet the diverse needs of learners."
UDL Evidence: UDL-infused lesson plans; student work showing varied expression modes; observation data
Criterion 3.5 (Assessment for Learning): "Assessment practices are varied and provide students with multiple ways to demonstrate learning."
UDL Evidence: Assessment menus/choice boards; rubrics for diverse products; formative feedback examples

content.text: WASC Category B: Curriculum, Instruction, and Assessment

steps: B1 (Rigorous and Relevant Standards-Based Curriculum): UDL maintains Cambridge rigor while increasing access
B2 (Equity and Access to Curriculum): UDL proactively removes barriers; supports EAL and SEN students
B3 (Instructional Practices): UDL is evidence-based pedagogy aligned with research on how students learn
Differentiated Learning (Pembelajaran Berdiferensiasi): UDL operationalizes the Merdeka Curriculum's call for personalized, student-centered learning
Inclusive Education: UDL supports government mandate (Permendikbud No. 70/2009) for inclusive schools accepting diverse learners
content.title: Indonesian National Education Requirements

steps: Berkebinekaan Global (Global Diversity): UDL curriculum includes diverse perspectives, honors students' cultural backgrounds
Mandiri (Independent): UDL promotes student agency, choice, and self-regulation
Gotong Royong (Collaboration): UDL engagement strategies emphasize cooperative learning
Access Arrangements: UDL documentation (e.g., "student's normal way of working") supports applications for exam accommodations (extra time, reader, scribe)
Inclusive Assessment Practices: Cambridge values varied assessment modes; UDL aligns with this philosophy
Centre Support Plans: Schools must demonstrate support for diverse learners; UDL provides systematic framework and evidence
content.title: Cambridge Centre Requirements

content.text: Learning Objective: Students will identify parts of a plant and explain their functions. UDL Design: Representation:

steps: Text: Simple textbook page with diagram and labels
Visual: Bring real plants to class; students observe and touch
Video: Animated video showing plant growth and functions (with English and Indonesian subtitles)
Interactive: Digital labeling activity on tablets (Quizlet, Kahoot)
content.title: Example 1: Grade 3 Cambridge Primary (Science—Plants)

steps: Option A: Draw and label plant diagram with written sentences explaining each part
Option B: Create 3D plant model (clay, paper) with labeled parts; oral explanation to teacher
Option C: Record video "tour" of plant, pointing to parts and explaining functions (All assessed on accuracy of labels, clarity of explanations, understanding of functions)
Hook: Plant a seed together; students predict what will happen
Choice: Students select which plant to study (flower, vegetable, tree)
Real-world: Discuss plants students see in their neighborhoods or eat at home (Indonesian vegetables—kangkung, bayam)

content.text: EAL Support: Vocabulary cards with images; sentence frame ("The \\ [part] helps the plant to \\ [function]"); partner with proficient English speaker.
Learning Objective: Analyze causes and key events leading to Indonesian independence in 1945. UDL Design: Representation:

steps: Text: Textbook chapter \+ primary source documents (Proclamation text, Sukarno speeches)
Visual: Timeline infographic; maps showing Japanese occupation; photo gallery of key figures
Audio: Recorded speeches or interviews with historians (Indonesian language available)
Multimedia: Documentary film clips
content.title: Example 2: Grade 8 Cambridge Lower Secondary (History—Indonesian Independence)

steps: Option A: Write analytical essay comparing Indonesian and Dutch perspectives
Option B: Create digital timeline with annotations explaining significance of each event

Option C: Perform historical role-play (student as Sukarno, Hatta, or independence fighter; explain motivations and actions) (All assessed on historical accuracy, analysis of causes, use of evidence, perspective-taking)
Hook: Discuss "What does independence mean to you?" Connect to personal freedoms students value.
Cultural relevance: Study Indonesian heroes; visit museum (field trip) or virtual tour
Current connections: Discuss how independence shaped modern Indonesia

content.text: EAL Support: Key terms glossary (English-Indonesian); graphic organizer for cause-effect analysis; sentence frames for essay ("One cause of independence was \\ because \\").
Learning Objective: Investigate factors affecting rates of chemical reactions; explain using collision theory. UDL Design: Representation:

steps: Text: Cambridge textbook chapter with equations and explanations
Visual: Animated simulations showing particle collisions at molecular level (PhET Simulations)
Practical: Hands-on experiments (temperature, concentration, surface area, catalyst)
Video: Recorded lecture explaining collision theory with visual aids
content.title: Example 3: Grade 10 IGCSE Chemistry (Rates of Reaction)

steps: Option A: Traditional lab report (hypothesis, method, results, analysis, conclusion)
Option B: Video demonstration of experiments with voice-over explanation and data analysis
